---
title: "Reader's Guide: Jegadeesh and Titman 1993 — The Paper That Named Momentum"
date: "2026-07-20"
---

# Reader's Guide: Jegadeesh and Titman 1993 {.title}

::: notes
Welcome to Week Five, lesson three, part one of eleven. This lesson is a reader's guide to one of the most consequential papers in empirical finance: Jegadeesh and Titman, nineteen ninety-three, "Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers," published in the Journal of Finance. Yesterday's guides put a price on risk — Fama and French said average returns line up with size and book-to-market. Those are efficient-markets papers. Today's paper broke that story open. It takes a strategy a teenage day-trader would scribble on a napkin — buy the stocks that went up, short the ones that went down — and shows it makes money, robustly, for decades. In this opening part we frame why this matters.
:::

## Why This Paper Is on the Syllabus

- Names the **momentum** effect in stock returns
- Strategy is almost insultingly simple
- A dumb rule beating the market is damaging
- Either a missing risk factor, or prices misbehave
- Thirty years later, the argument is unresolved

::: notes
This is the paper that named momentum. It matters not because the strategy is exotic — it is almost insultingly simple — but because of what its success implies. If a rule this dumb beats the market after you adjust for known risk, then one of two things must be true. Either there is a risk factor nobody had written down yet, or prices are not impounding information the way the efficient-markets hypothesis says they do. Thirty years later we still argue about which. That unresolved argument is exactly why this paper is on the syllabus. It is a clean, replicable anomaly that forces you to take a side and defend it with evidence. A simple rule that works is far more damaging to a theory than a complicated one, because there is nowhere to hide a degree of freedom.
:::

## How We Read It

- Tables first, in a deliberate order
- Hunt for where the result could break
- Use the fixed Reader's-Guide anatomy from Week Five
- Believe the finding only after stress-testing it

::: notes
We read this paper the professional way: tables first, in a deliberate order, hunting for where the result could break before we let ourselves believe it. The fixed reader's-guide anatomy from the Week Five reading pack drives the sections that follow. We start with the research question, then the identification strategy, then the data, then a table-by-table reading order. After that we ask what is clever about the design and, just as important, what is vulnerable about it. Finally we lay out replication exercises and the questions a referee would write back with. By the end you should be able to explain not just what momentum is, but why its very simplicity is the strongest part of the evidence — and where a skeptic would still push back hard.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Jegadeesh and Titman, nineteen ninety-three, named **momentum**: buy past winners, short past losers. Its simplicity is the point — a dumb rule that beats risk-adjusted returns is a clean anomaly that forces you to pick a side. Next: the precise research question.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part one: Jegadeesh and Titman, nineteen ninety-three, is the paper that named momentum — buy past winners, short past losers. Its power comes from its simplicity. A rule that needs only past returns, yet beats the market after risk adjustment, is a direct challenge to the efficient-markets hypothesis, and the resulting argument is still live today. We read it tables-first, hunting for breakage. In part two we state the precise research question the authors set out to answer, and pin down why the exact horizon they chose is the whole game.
:::
